verdict_reasoning: BAX is canonically a cytosolic/mitochondrial pro-apoptotic BCL2-family protein that inserts into the mitochondrial outer membrane upon apoptotic activation. However, BAX has well-documented cell-death-induced translocation to the plasma membrane outer leaflet during apoptosis and immunogenic cell death — multiple studies show BAX surface exposure on apoptotic cells, where it is accessible to antibodies from outside the cell. Additionally, tumor cells (oncogenic/stress states) display BAX on their outer leaflet constitutively or under stress. This fits `cell_state_induced` under apoptosis/ICD-related PM translocation. The protein's dominant resting state is cytosolic/mitochondrial, but documented outer-leaflet exposure during cell death qualifies it as `contextual`. No stable surface display in baseline non-stressed cells; no TM anchor of its own at rest — insertion is activation-dependent.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Extent and wash-resistance of BAX outer-leaflet PM exposure during apoptosis vs. mitochondrial-membrane insertion; whether the PM surface pool is robust enough for therapeutic targeting vs. being an incidental apoptotic marker.
